subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 12! In this unit you will explore the world of three-dimensional shapes – the solid shapes that take up space all around you. When Amina stacks tins of tomato paste at her family stall in the Hargeisa market, when Yusuf helps fold a flat sheet of cardboard into a box, or when Hassan looks up at the dome of the masjid, they are all meeting 3D shapes. These shapes are not flat like the drawings on a page; you can hold them, turn them, and look at them from every side.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, and whenever you can, pick up a real object – a tin, a box, a ball, a party hat – and check the shape with your own hands. Mathematics about solid shapes is much easier when you touch and turn the shapes themselves. Your AI tutor is available day and night to explain a step again, invent more practice, or check your counting. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter? Understanding 3D shapes helps you make sense of the built world. Architects who design the tall towers of Nairobi, engineers who build bridges, and craftspeople who make furniture all think carefully about solid shapes. People who design packaging use nets every single day: before a box of dates or a carton of camel milk is filled, it starts life as a flat net that is folded and glued. When you understand shapes and nets, you are learning to think like a designer, a builder and a problem-solver.

As you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you, and if you can, gather a few empty boxes and tins to examine. Mathematics is not a subject you learn only by reading – you must do it. Point to the faces, run your finger along the edges, and press each vertex. Say the numbers out loud. If you miscount at first, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every idea in this unit becomes clear with a little handling and a little patience.
Identify and name common 3D shapes: cubes, cuboids, prisms, pyramids, cylinders, cones and spheres.
Describe 3D shapes using the correct words faces, edges and vertices.
Count the faces, edges and vertices of a solid shape carefully and accurately.
Classify 3D shapes as polyhedra (flat faces only) or non-polyhedra (with a curved surface).
Recognise the 2D shapes that make up the faces of a 3D shape.
Understand what a net is and how it folds to make a 3D shape.
Identify which net folds to make a given 3D shape, and begin to draw simple nets.

concepts.title: What Makes a Shape 3D?
concepts.explanation: A two-dimensional (2D) shape is flat. A square, a triangle and a circle are all 2D – they have length and width, but no thickness. You can draw them fully on a flat page. A three-dimensional (3D) shape is different: it has length, width and height. It is solid, it takes up space, and you can pick it up and look at it from many sides. The drawing of a box on paper is 2D, but the real box you can hold is 3D. Think about a football and a circle drawn on the ground. The circle is flat – you can stand a stone right on…
concepts.example: To describe 3D shapes carefully, mathematicians use three special words. Learning to see these three features on any solid is the most important skill in the whole unit:

concepts.title: The Common 3D Shapes and Their Properties
concepts.explanation: Let us meet the family of common 3D shapes one by one. For each shape, we count its faces, edges and vertices, and we name a few everyday objects that share its shape. Try to find a real example of each as you read. A cuboid is a box shape. It has 6 rectangular faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices. Opposite faces are the same size. A tea box, a brick, a book and most cartons at the market are cuboids. The cuboid is probably the most common 3D shape in daily life because it stacks neatly and packs together with no wasted…
concepts.example: A cube is a special cuboid where all 6 faces are squares of exactly the same size. Like the cuboid, it has 12 edges and 8 vertices. A dice, a sugar cube and a Rubik's cube are all cubes. Every cube is a cuboid, but not…

concepts.title: Counting Faces, Edges and Vertices Carefully
concepts.explanation: Counting the parts of a solid sounds easy, but it is very easy to miss one or count one twice. The secret is to be systematic – to count in the same careful order every time, rather than jumping around the shape. Here is a reliable method for counting the faces of a cuboid. Count the face on top, then the face on the bottom – that is 2. Now count the front and the back – that is 4. Finally the left and the right – that is 6. By pairing opposite faces you never lose track. A cuboid always has these 6 faces.
concepts.example: To count edges, work around the shape in groups. On a cuboid, count the 4 edges around the top, then the 4 edges around the bottom, then the 4 upright edges joining top to bottom. That gives 4 + 4 + 4 = 12 edges.…

concepts.title: Sorting Shapes – Polyhedra, Prisms and Pyramids
concepts.explanation: Mathematicians love to sort shapes into groups, because shapes in the same group share useful properties. The first big sort is between shapes with flat faces and shapes with a curved surface. A polyhedron (plural polyhedra) is a solid with flat faces only – no curved surfaces. Cubes, cuboids, prisms and pyramids are all polyhedra.
concepts.example: A non-polyhedron is a solid with at least one curved surface. Cylinders, cones and spheres belong here.

concepts.title: Patterns in Prisms and Pyramids
concepts.explanation: Once you have sorted shapes into prisms and pyramids, a wonderful thing happens: you can predict their properties without even seeing the shape. This is because prisms and pyramids follow neat patterns based on the number of sides of their base. We use the letter n to stand for the number of sides of the base. Number of faces = n + 2 (the two ends plus one rectangle for each side).
concepts.example: Number of edges = 3n (n edges around each end, plus n edges joining them).

concepts.title: Euler's Famous Formula
concepts.explanation: Long ago a mathematician named Euler (say it ‘OY-ler’) noticed something amazing about every polyhedron – every solid with flat faces. He found that if you take the number of faces (F), add the number of vertices (V), and subtract the number of edges (E), you always get 2. We write this rule as: Let us check it on a cube. A cube has F = 6 faces, V = 8 vertices and E = 12 edges. Put these into the formula: 6 + 8 − 12 = 14 − 12 = 2. It works! Now try a square-based pyramid: F = 5, V = 5, E = 8, so 5 + 5 − 8 = 10 − 8…
concepts.example: Euler's formula is more than a curiosity – it is a helpful checking tool. If you count the faces, edges and vertices of a polyhedron and the formula does not give 2, then you know you have miscounted somewhere and…

explorations.prompt: Write the number of faces, edges and vertices for: a) cube b) cuboid c) sphere.
explorations.answer: 1) a) 6, 12, 8 b) 6, 12, 8 c) 1 face, 0 edges, 0 vertices.
explorations.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.
explorations.explanation: 1) a) 6, 12, 8 b) 6, 12, 8 c) 1 face, 0 edges, 0 vertices.

explorations.answer: Cylinder, cone and sphere have curved surfaces.

methods.title: How to count faces, edges and vertices
methods.example: How many faces, edges and vertices does a cube have?
steps: Count faces in opposite pairs so you never miss one.
Count edges in groups (around the top, around the bottom, then the joining edges).
Count vertices by touching each corner once.
Check your totals with Euler's formula if the shape has flat faces.

methods.title: How to match a net to a shape
methods.example: What 2D shapes make up the faces of a triangular prism?
steps: Count how many faces are in the net.
Identify the 2D shape of each face (square, rectangle, triangle, circle).
Picture the faces folding up with no gaps or overlaps.
Name the solid that has exactly those faces.

methods.title: Using the prism pattern
methods.example: How many faces, edges and vertices does a hexagonal prism have?
steps: Solution: A hexagon has 6 sides, so n = 6.
Vertices = 2n = 2 × 6 = 12.

methods.title: Using the pyramid pattern
methods.example: How many faces, edges and vertices does a pentagonal pyramid have?
steps: Solution: A pentagon has 5 sides, so n = 5.
Vertices = n + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6.

methods.title: Checking Euler's formula
methods.example: Does Euler's formula work for a triangular prism?
steps: Solution: A triangular prism has F = 5 faces, V = 6 vertices and E = 9 edges.
Put them into F + V − E: 5 + 6 − 9 = 11 − 9 = 2.
Yes – the answer is 2, so Euler's formula works.

methods.title: Matching a net to a shape
methods.example: A net is made of 1 square with a triangle joined to each of its four sides. What shape does it fold into?
steps: Solution: Count the faces: 1 square and 4 triangles, which is 5 faces.
A shape with 1 square base and 4 triangular faces meeting at a point is a square-based pyramid.
Folding the four triangles up brings their tips together at the apex.

workedExamples.title: Counting a cube's parts
workedExamples.prompt: How many faces, edges and vertices does a cube have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count the faces in pairs – top and bottom, front and back, left and right – giving 6 faces. Count the edges in groups – 4 around the top, 4 around the bottom, 4 upright – giving 4 + 4 + 4 = 12 edges. Count the vertices – 4 on top and 4 on the bottom – giving 8 vertices. A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices.

workedExamples.title: Naming faces
workedExamples.prompt: What 2D shapes make up the faces of a triangular prism?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A triangular prism has 2 triangular faces at its ends and 3 rectangular faces around its sides. So its faces are made of triangles and rectangles – 5 faces in all.

workedExamples.title: Using the prism pattern
workedExamples.prompt: How many faces, edges and vertices does a hexagonal prism have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A hexagon has 6 sides, so n = 6. Faces = n + 2 = 6 + 2 = 8. Edges = 3n = 3 × 6 = 18. Vertices = 2n = 2 × 6 = 12. A hexagonal prism has 8 faces, 18 edges and 12 vertices.

workedExamples.title: Using the pyramid pattern
workedExamples.prompt: How many faces, edges and vertices does a pentagonal pyramid have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A pentagon has 5 sides, so n = 5. Faces = n + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6. Edges = 2n = 2 × 5 = 10. Vertices = n + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6. A pentagonal pyramid has 6 faces, 10 edges and 6 vertices.

workedExamples.title: Checking Euler's formula
workedExamples.prompt: Does Euler's formula work for a triangular prism?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A triangular prism has F = 5 faces, V = 6 vertices and E = 9 edges. Put them into F + V − E: 5 + 6 − 9 = 11 − 9 = 2. Yes – the answer is 2, so Euler's formula works.

workedExamples.title: Matching a net to a shape
workedExamples.prompt: A net is made of 1 square with a triangle joined to each of its four sides. What shape does it fold into?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count the faces: 1 square and 4 triangles, which is 5 faces. A shape with 1 square base and 4 triangular faces meeting at a point is a square-based pyramid. Folding the four triangles up brings their tips together at the apex.

workedExamples.title: Total length of edges
workedExamples.prompt: A cube has edges 5 cm long. What is the total length of all its edges combined?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A cube has 12 edges, and each is 5 cm. Total length = 12 × 5 = 60 cm. First recall the number of edges, then multiply by the length of one edge.

workedExamples.title: Reasoning about a mystery shape
workedExamples.prompt: A polyhedron has 2 circular ends... wait – a shape has 5 faces, 9 edges and 6 vertices. Name it.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First check Euler's formula: 5 + 6 − 9 = 2, so it is a valid polyhedron. Now think which shape has these numbers. A triangular prism has exactly 5 faces, 9 edges and 6 vertices, so the shape is a triangular prism. Matching the counts to a known shape is a powerful way to identify solids.

workedExamples.title: For each net, name the 3D shape
workedExamples.prompt: For each net, name the 3D shape: a) 6 squares b) 1 square and 4 triangles c) 2 circles and 1 rectangle.
workedExamples.solution: a) cube b) square-based pyramid c) cylinder.

workedExamples.title: Check Euler's formula (F + V −
workedExamples.prompt: Check Euler's formula (F + V − E) for: a) cube b) triangular prism c) tetrahedron.

workedExamples.title: Which shape is described
workedExamples.prompt: Which shape is described: a) 5 faces, 8 edges, 5 vertices b) 2 circular faces and 1 curved surface?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: An octagonal prism has an 8-sided base. Use the pattern to find its faces, edges and vertices, then verify with Euler's formula.

activities.title: Shape Hunt Around the Home
activities.materials: a notebook and pencil, and permission to look around your home.
steps: Walk through your home and find one object for each shape: cube, cuboid, cylinder, cone, sphere.
Write down the object and the shape it matches (for example, ‘tin of milk – cylinder’).
For each flat-faced object, count its faces, edges and vertices.
Which shape was easiest to find? Which was hardest? Talk about why.
Keep your list – it shows how 3D shapes are hiding all around you every day.

activities.title: Unfold a Box to Find Its Net
activities.materials: a clean empty cardboard box (a cuboid), such as a tea or dates box, and scissors (ask an adult to help).
steps: Carefully cut along some of the edges of the box, but not all, so it can open out flat.
Lay the opened box flat on a table. This flat pattern is its net.
Count the rectangles in the net. How many are there? Which ones are the same size?
Fold the net back up into the box to see how the net becomes a solid again.
You have just discovered the net of a cuboid – exactly what a factory starts with when making a box.

activities.title: Build a Cube From a Net
activities.materials: paper or thin card, a ruler, a pencil, scissors and tape.
steps: Draw 6 equal squares in a cross or plus-sign pattern: a row of four squares with one above and one below.
Add small tabs on some edges to help you glue or tape.
Cut out the whole net in one piece.
Fold along every line and tape the edges together to make a cube. Count its faces, edges and vertices.
Challenge: there are 11 different nets that fold into a cube. Can you find and build a second one?

activities.title: The Faces-Edges-Vertices Game
activities.materials: paper cards, each with the name of a 3D shape, and a set of real objects.
steps: Shuffle the shape cards and place them face down.
Each player turns over one card and names the faces, edges and vertices of that shape.
Check the answer using an object or the table in the Lesson.
Score one point for each of faces, edges and vertices you get right. Highest score wins!
This game trains you to remember shape properties quickly and accurately.

activities.title: Euler's Formula Investigation
activities.materials: the models you built, or the property table from the Lesson, and paper.
steps: Choose three different polyhedra (for example, cube, triangular prism, square-based pyramid).
For each, write down the faces (F), vertices (V) and edges (E).
Work out F + V − E for each shape.
Do you always get 2? Try two more shapes and see if the rule ever breaks for a flat-faced solid.
You are investigating a rule discovered by a famous mathematician – and checking it yourself, just like a real scientist.

activities.title: Design a Package (Family or Group Task)
activities.materials: paper or card, a ruler, scissors, tape, and a small item to package (such as a handful of dates).
steps: Choose a shape for your package: cuboid, triangular prism, or square-based pyramid.
Draw the net for your chosen shape, adding tabs for gluing.
Cut out and fold your net to make the package, then place your item inside.
Explain to your family why you chose that shape. Was it easy to fold? Did it hold the item well?
Real packaging designers do exactly this – they choose a shape, draw a net, and test whether it works.

rules.title: By the end of this unit you
rules.text: By the end of this unit you will be able to name common 3D shapes such as cubes, cuboids, prisms, pyramids, cylinders, cones and spheres. You will describe them using the correct mathematical words – faces, edges and vertices – and count each of these accurately. You will learn how a flat pattern called a net folds up to make a solid shape, and how to tell which net makes which shape. You will even discover clever patterns and a famous rule, called Euler's formula, that connects the faces, edges and vertices of every flat-faced solid.

rules.title: Long ago a mathematician named Euler (say
rules.text: Long ago a mathematician named Euler (say it ‘OY-ler’) noticed something amazing about every polyhedron – every solid with flat faces. He found that if you take the number of faces (F), add the number of vertices (V), and subtract the number of edges (E), you always get 2. We write this rule as:

rules.title: Euler's formula is more than a curiosity
rules.text: Euler's formula is more than a curiosity – it is a helpful checking tool. If you count the faces, edges and vertices of a polyhedron and the formula does not give 2, then you know you have miscounted somewhere and should go back and check. Careful mathematicians use Euler's formula to catch their own mistakes. Note that the rule works only for polyhedra with flat faces; curved shapes like cylinders and cones do not follow it.

terms: A solid with length, width and height
A flat or curved surface of a solid
The straight line where two faces meet
A corner point where edges meet
A solid with flat faces only
Same cross-section all the way through
A base with triangles meeting at a point
A flat pattern that folds into a solid
commonMistakes: Point to a whole side, then to a meeting line
A sphere has 1 curved surface, no edges, no vertices
Forgetting the apex of a pyramid
Count base corners AND the point at the top
Triangular and pentagonal prisms are prisms too

realProblems.answer: 3 pieces – two circles and one rectangle.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3 pieces – two circles and one rectangle.

realProblems.prompt: A square-based pyramid model has a base of side 6 cm. How many faces, edges and vertices does it have, and does it satisfy Euler's formula?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: An octagonal prism has an 8-sided base. Use the pattern to find its faces, edges and vertices, then verify with Euler's formula.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: An octagonal prism has an 8-sided base. Use the pattern to find its faces, edges and vertices, then verify with Euler's formula.
A heptagonal pyramid has a 7-sided base. Find its faces, edges and vertices using the pyramid pattern.
Hassan says every shape with 6 faces must be a cube. Is he right? Explain with an example.
A polyhedron has 6 faces and 12 edges. Use Euler's formula to work out how many vertices it has, then name a shape it could be.
Explain the key idea in Patterns in Prisms and Pyramids.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Once you have sorted shapes into prisms and pyramids, a wonderful thing happens: you can predict their properties without even seeing the shape. This is because prisms and pyramids follow neat patterns based on the number of sides of their base. We use the letter n to stand for the number of sides of the base. Number of faces = n + 2 (the two ends plus one rectangle for each side).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Euler's Famous Formula.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Long ago a mathematician named Euler (say it ‘OY-ler’) noticed something amazing about every polyhedron – every solid with flat faces. He found that if you take the number of faces (F), add the number of vertices (V), and subtract the number of edges (E), you always get 2. We write this rule as: Let us check it on a cube. A cube has F = 6 faces, V = 8 vertices and E = 12 edges. Put these into the formula: 6 + 8 − 12 = 14 − 12 = 2. It works! Now try a square-based pyramid: F = 5, V = 5, E = 8, so 5 + 5 − 8 = 10 − 8…

questions.question: What does “3D shape” mean?

A flat or curved surface of a solid
The straight line where two faces meet
A corner point where edges meet

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, and whenever you can, pick up a real object – a tin, a box, a ball, a party hat – and check the shape with your own hands. Mathematics about solid shapes is much easier when you touch and turn the shapes themselves. Your AI tutor is available day and night to explain a step again, invent more practice, or check your counting. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter? Understanding 3D shapes helps you make sense of the built world. Architects who design the tall towers of Nairobi, engineers who build bridges, and craftspeople who make furniture all think carefully about solid shapes. People who design packaging use nets every single day: before a box of dates or a carton of camel milk is filled, it starts life as a flat net that is folded and glued. When you understand shapes and nets, you are learning to think like a designer, a builder and a problem-solver.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you, and if you can, gather a few empty boxes and tins to examine. Mathematics is not a subject you learn only by reading – you must do it. Point to the faces, run your finger along the edges, and press each vertex. Say the numbers out loud. If you miscount at first, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every idea in this unit becomes clear with a little handling and a little patience.
I can identify and name common 3D shapes: cubes, cuboids, prisms, pyramids, cylinders, cones and spheres.
I can describe 3D shapes using the correct words faces, edges and vertices.
I can count the faces, edges and vertices of a solid shape carefully and accurately.
I can classify 3D shapes as polyhedra (flat faces only) or non-polyhedra (with a curved surface).
I can recognise the 2D shapes that make up the faces of a 3D shape.
